statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Andrei has a huge pile of $ n $ apples. He can divide the pile into two smaller piles: if there are $ x $ apples in the pile, he will get piles with $ \lfloor \frac{x}{2} \rfloor $ $ ^{\text{∗}} $ and $ \lceil \frac{x}{2} \rceil $ $ ^{\text{†}} $ apples. This division takes Andrei $ 1 $ minute.

Andrei wants to eat $ k $ apples, but he doesn't want to count them at all. That is why he wants to obtain a pile that contains exactly $ k $ apples. Determine whether it is possible to achieve this by performing pile divisions. If it is possible, find the minimum possible time for Andrei to obtain a pile with exactly $ k $ apples.

$ ^{\text{∗}} $ $ \lfloor \frac{x}{2} \rfloor $ — the largest integer $ \le \frac{x}{2} $ .

$ ^{\text{†}} $ $ \lceil \frac{x}{2} \rceil $ — the smallest integer $ \ge \frac{x}{2} $ .

Input Format
Each test consists of several test cases. The first line contains a single integer $ t $ $ (1 \le t \le 10^4) $ — the number of test cases. The following lines describe the test cases.

In the only line of each test case, two integers $ n $ and $ k $ are given — the number of apples in the huge pile and the number of apples that Andrei wants to obtain in one pile $ (1 \le n, k \le 10^9) $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output $ -1 $ if it is impossible to obtain a pile with exactly $ k $ apples. Otherwise, output the minimum possible time required to obtain such a pile.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
10 3
11 5
21 4
1000000000 1
Output
2
1
-1
29

Note
In the first test case, after the first division, two piles of $ 5 $ apples will be created. If one of them is divided, it will result in piles with $ 2 $ and $ 3 $ apples, so the answer is $ 2 $ .

In the second test case, if the pile is divided into two, it will result in piles with $ 5 $ and $ 6 $ apples, so the answer is $ 1 $ .

In the third test case, it is only possible to obtain piles with $ 1 $ , $ 2 $ , $ 3 $ , $ 5 $ , $ 6 $ , $ 10 $ , $ 11 $ , or $ 21 $ apples, so the answer is $ -1 $ .

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content:
Why is brute force ineffective? How can it be improved?
Answer
Because the same numbers are visited multiple times.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content:
How many distinct values can be obtained starting from the number $$$n$$$?
Answer
$$$O(\log n)$$$.

solutions.title: Editorial
solutions.content:
2184C - Huge Pile
Let's construct a
decision tree
starting from the number $$$n$$$: from each vertex $$$x$$$, edges lead to $$$\lfloor \frac{x}{2} \rfloor$$$ and $$$\lceil \frac{x}{2} \rceil$$$. The process stops at the values $$$0$$$ and $$$1$$$, since $$$k \ge 1$$$.
It is claimed that the number of
distinct
values in such a tree is small. Let the $$$i$$$-th layer of the tree be the set of vertices located at depth $$$i$$$ from the root, where the number $$$n$$$ is located. Let the minimum value on the $$$i$$$-th layer be $$$l_i$$$, and the maximum be $$$r_i$$$. Then $$$l_0 = r_0 = n$$$.
Note that for the next layer, the following estimates hold: $$$l_{i+1} \ge \lfloor \frac{l_i}{2} \rfloor \ge \frac{l_i-1}{2}$$$ and $$$r_{i+1} \le \lceil \frac{r_i}{2} \rceil \le \frac{r_i+1}{2}$$$.
Let's estimate the number of integers on the $$$(i+1)$$$-th layer using the difference $$$r_{i+1} - l_{i+1} + 1$$$: $$$$$$r_{i+1} - l_{i+1} + 1 \le \frac{r_i + 1}{2} - \frac{l_i - 1}{2} + 1 = \frac{r_i - l_i}{2} + 2$$$$$$
Let $$$d_i = r_i - l_i + 1$$$ be the number of values in the range on layer $$$i$$$. Then $$$d_{i+1} \le \frac{d_i - 1}{2} + 2$$$. Let's analyze this relation:
1. If $$$d_i = 1$$$, then $$$d_{i+1} \le 0.5 \cdot 0 + 2 = 2$$$.
2. If $$$d_i = 2$$$, then $$$d_{i+1} \le 0.5 \cdot 1 + 2 = 2.5 \Rightarrow d_{i+1} \le 2$$$ (since the number is an integer).
Thus, if a layer had no more than $$$2$$$ distinct values, then the next layer will also have
no more than 2
. Since there is only one value ($$$n$$$) on the zero layer, there will be no more than two distinct numbers on each subsequent layer.
There will be $$$O(\log n)$$$ layers in the tree in total, which means the total number of distinct vertices will be $$$O(\log n)$$$.
To solve the problem, it is sufficient to find the minimum depth $$$i$$$ at which the number $$$k$$$ occurs among the vertices of the $$$i$$$-th layer. This can be done either by iterating through the layers and constructing the values for the current layer based on the previous one, or by using recursion with memoization (storing results for each number $$$x$$$).
